mathml_representations_list: <mstyle id="custom-group-0" class="custom-group"><mi id="mi_167">∂</mi><msub id="msub_24"><mrow><mi id="mi_168">Ω</mi></mrow><mrow><mi id="mi_169">N</mi></mrow></msub></mstyle>

<mstyle id="custom-group-3" class="custom-group"><msup id="msup_7"><mrow><mi id="mi_145">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mrow><mo class="bigl" fence="true">(</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="script" id="mi_146">T</mi><mo>;</mo><mstyle id="custom-group-2" class="custom-group"><msubsup id="msubsup_4"><mrow><mi id="mi_147">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msubsup><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi id="mi_148">Ω</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></mstyle></mrow><mo class="bigr" fence="true">)</mo></mrow></mstyle>

concepts_list.name: u_field
concepts_list.description: The primary unknown field representing the displacement in the wave equation.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol 'u' appears multiple times as the dependent variable in the wave equation and in the weak formulation, such as in 'u(t,x)', '∂²u/∂t²', and in the test function pairing. It is explicitly described as the dependent variable in the initial equations and the weak solution.

concepts_list.name: w_test_function
concepts_list.description: A test function used in the weak formulation to enforce the variational form of the wave equation.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol 'w' is repeatedly used in the weak formulation as a test function, appearing in integrals with 'u', and in the domain of the Sobolev space. It is explicitly mentioned as part of the test function space and used in the duality pairing with the second time derivative of 'u'.

concepts_list.name: rho_mass_density
concepts_list.description: The mass per unit volume, a material property in the wave equation governing the inertial term.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol 'ρ' is explicitly defined as 'the positive mass per unit volume' in the text, and appears in the time derivative term of the wave equation and in the weak form as a coefficient in the duality pairing.

concepts_list.name: T_tensile_stress
concepts_list.description: The tensile pre-stress as load per unit surface area, appearing in the constitutive relation of the wave equation.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol 'T' is explicitly defined as 'the positive tensile pre-stress as load per unit surface area' and appears in both the strong and weak forms, including in the gradient term and the interface conditions.

concepts_list.name: Omega_domain
concepts_list.description: The main spatial computational domain in which the wave equation is defined.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol 'Ω' is repeatedly used to denote the d-dimensional spatial domain, partitioned into subdomains, and is the domain of integration in the weak form. It is explicitly described as the spatial domain and appears in integrals over Ω and its boundaries.

concepts_list.name: t_time
concepts_list.description: The time variable in the time-dependent wave equation and weak formulation.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol 't' appears in the time derivative terms (∂²u/∂t²), in the initial conditions, and in the temporal domain notation, indicating it is a time variable in the evolution of the solution.

concepts_list.name: Gamma_interface
concepts_list.description: The collection of interfaces between subdomains where transmission conditions are enforced.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol 'Γ' is explicitly defined as 'the collection of interfaces between the subdomains Ωi', and appears in the jump conditions and integration over Γ in the weak form, indicating it is a domain boundary between subdomains.

concepts_list.name: d_Omega
concepts_list.description: The differential element for integration over the spatial domain Ω.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol 'dΩ' appears in integrals over Ω and Γ, indicating it is the integration element in the weak formulation, as per standard notation in variational methods.

concepts_list.name: d_Gamma
concepts_list.description: The differential element for integration over the boundary interface Γ.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol 'dΓ' appears in the weak formulation as the integration element over the interface Γ, where boundary conditions are applied, indicating it is the surface element for integration on the interface.

concepts_list.name: g_boundary_load
concepts_list.description: The boundary load applied on the Neumann boundary ∂ΩN.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol 'g' is explicitly defined in the Neumann condition as 'the boundary load' and appears in the weak form as a term involving 'g w dΓ', indicating it is a given boundary condition.

concepts_list.name: H_0_1_Omega
concepts_list.description: The Sobolev space of functions with zero normal trace on the boundary, used for the test and solution functions.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol 'H⁰₁(Ω)' is explicitly defined as the Sobolev space of functions satisfying Dirichlet boundary conditions and appears in the weak formulation as the space for both the solution and test functions.

concepts_list.name: H_1_temporal
concepts_list.description: The space of functions in H¹(Ω) valued over time, used to define the solution space in the weak formulation.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol 'H¹(T; H⁰₁(Ω))' is explicitly defined as the space of H⁰₁(Ω)-valued functions that are smooth in time, and is used to describe the solution space in the weak formulation.

concepts_list.name: H_minus1_dual
concepts_list.description: The dual space of H⁻¹(Ω), used for the duality pairing with the second time derivative of u.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol 'H⁻¹(Ω)' is explicitly mentioned as the dual space of H¹(Ω), and appears in the duality pairing between the second time derivative of u and the test function w, indicating it is the dual space in the weak formulation.

concepts_list.name: cal_T
concepts_list.description: The tensile stress operator used in the gradient term and interface conditions.
concepts_list.justification: The symbol 'T' appears in the context of ∇·(T∇u) and in the jump condition [T ∂n u], indicating it is used as a coefficient in a differential operator. Although not explicitly named as an operator, its role in the gradient and interface conditions supports its classification as an operator.
